items.title: Who is really to blame?
items.header: If a woman’s life has been a struggle, an endless battle of endurance, is it not possible that the collective female psyche has, in turn, shaped a world where men are sent to fight and die?
items.text:
Is war, at its core, an unconscious vengeance of women against men? A price men pay for their historical dominance, for their use of the female body, for their demand to reproduce? Throughout history, women have suffered—physically, emotionally, and socially—often silenced, denied autonomy, and reduced to their reproductive roles.

If a woman’s life has been a struggle, an endless battle of endurance, is it not possible that the collective female psyche has, in turn, shaped a world where men are sent to fight and die? A body for a body so to say.

Carl Jung, in his theory of the collective unconscious, spoke of archetypes—deep, inherited patterns of thought that shape human behavior. Among these is the anima, the feminine aspect within the male psyche, and the animus, the masculine aspect within the female psyche. The unresolved tensions between these forces can manifest in society at large. If men have historically exerted dominance over women, suppressing their voices and autonomy, could this oppression have unconsciously woven itself into the fabric of civilization, ensuring that men, in return, pay their price through war?

Simone de Beauvoir, in The Second Sex (1949), described how society has long constructed woman as the "Other", defining her existence in relation to men. She writes:

"Man thinks of his body as a direct and normal connection with the world, which he appropriates as he sees fit. Whereas woman’s body is always something other than herself, a constraint, a prison, an object for the use of another."

If men have historically seen women as vessels and mere tools or objects for their own pleasure, reproduction, or power, could it be that war is, in part, a reflection of this imbalance? That the very society men have built, one where dominance is power, inevitably turns back upon them?

As Virginia Woolf wrote in Three Guineas (1938):

"As a woman, I have no country. As a woman, my country is the whole world."

Women, historically excluded from political power, have rarely been the architects of war, yet they have often been the silent sustainers of the system that sends men to fight. Mothers who raise sons, wives who encourage ambition, societies that reward aggression in men—all may unknowingly contribute to the cycle of war, reinforcing a system that ensures men are both the rulers and the sacrificed pawns.

But if war is, in some unconscious way, the retribution for the suffering of women, then the real question is: can this cycle be broken?

If society were to reject the ideal of power through dominance, if, at once, all the women refused to raise boys into warriors, and if men ceased to seek their worth through conquest together with humiliation and the exploitation of women, could we finally evolve beyond this ancient exchange of suffering?

What I want to tell is that nothing happens without reason. Every action creates a ripple that eventually returns to us, often in ways we never expect. This is the law of karma—the idea that the energy we put into the world, whether positive or negative, will return to us in kind. If we continue to perpetuate harm, it will come back in forms we might not anticipate, but it will always return. Just as we sow, so shall we reap.

Let's not forget Dr. Don Shirley from the movie Green Book:

"You never win with violence. You only win when you maintain your dignity."

items.title: Deep into the darkness
items.header: To truly understand the darkness, you must experience it. But once you've seen it, it's deepest, you no longer wish to stay there—your soul yearns for the light. But why? Does it work this way?
items.text:
Why must a person fall in order to see beauty? Why must one dive into the abyss, only to turn around after confronting its darkness? If light, simplicity, and the quiet wonders of existence—the falling snow, the flight of birds, the rhythm of running water—are already there, why are they not enough?

It seems humanity is wired to learn through loss, to awaken only after destruction. We do not cherish peace until we have known war. We do not value stillness until we have been consumed by chaos. A child is told that fire burns, yet only after feeling its heat does he understand both its danger and its beauty. Is suffering the only teacher we trust?

Friedrich Nietzsche once wrote:

"Man is something that shall be overcome. What have you done to overcome him?"

The world does not move forward in peace. It learns through struggle, through the ruins of its own making. Humanity devours itself—feeding on pride, greed, and violence—until the consequences become unbearable. And only then, standing on the edge of collapse, does it begin to reflect, to change, if change is still possible. Will we always need destruction to open our eyes? Will understanding always come too late?

Carl Jung believed in the necessity of confronting our darkness:

"No tree, it is said, can grow to heaven unless its roots reach down to hell."

Perhaps suffering deepens our perception. Perhaps those who have touched despair can see the world more clearly, recognizing the fragile beauty in the simple things—the snow, the birds, the water. But why must pain be the gateway to wisdom?

Must humanity always burn before it learns to respect the fire?

I hope there are still those who cannot accept this. Those who cannot bear to witness the destruction humanity brings to everything it touches. From the very beginning, history bleeds.

Yuval Noah Harari laid this truth bare:

"Homo sapiens is the deadliest species in the annals of planet Earth."

We have slaughtered animals, erased other human species, and carried conquest in our veins. Are we still continuing? Slavs, Asians, Jews, Africans, Indigenous peoples—how much more? How many more names must be added to history’s long list of victims?

And for those who understand what I am writing—if we do not accept this path, then why have we chosen this time? Why have we arrived here, in this moment?

And for those who understand what I am writing—if we do not accept this path, then why have we chosen this time? Why have we arrived here, in this moment?

items.title: The Illusion of Power and Self-Esteem
items.header: The wars have been the ultimate part of the human history. Elimiting other species, similar or not, bigger, and out of what? Rage? Fear? Low self-esteem?
items.text:
War has been an ultimate part of human history—an endless pattern of destruction, where one species, similar or not, seeks to eliminate the other. But what drives this? Rage? Fear? A deep-seated insecurity?

I fled the war because I refused to be part of it. People, in their masses, are ruled by those who hold money, status, and power. The average person can speculate on ideals, discuss them, and even believe they understand them. Yet, when the moment arrives to fight for those ideals—without well-crafted belief to prepare them—many hesitate. Many will instinctively step back, pushing others to the front lines instead. They will align themselves with those who act, borrowing their courage to boost their own self-esteem.

Erich Maria Remarque in All Quiet on the Western Front wrote the bare truth:

"The most terrible thing is that the war is only really a matter of the few who don't fight, but simply give orders. The others must fight and die without knowing the reason why."

I would only amend this slightly by adding "without knowing the REAL reason why." I doubt that one will go fighting for someone's else money and business. But for ideals, years-long built propaganda of some greatness, "truth", fame and collective enemy. This is "noble" to believe in such and absolutely this give one's little ego some significance.

Alfred Adler, the father of individual psychology, argued that self-esteem is the driving force behind human behavior. He believed that people are constantly striving to overcome feelings of inferiority—what he called the inferiority complex—often compensating for their fears by seeking power over others.

"The greater the feeling of inferiority that has been experienced, the more powerful is the urge to conquest and the more violent the emotional agitation." (Adler, Understanding Human Nature, 1927).

If war serves as a stage for proving one's superiority, then it is not courage that fuels it, but insecurity disguised as strength.

Why, then, do people believe that fighting and destroying others will make them mightier? Psychologists like Erich Fromm argued that destructiveness is a reaction to powerlessness. When individuals feel small, helpless, or controlled, they may attempt to restore a sense of dominance through aggression.

In his book The Anatomy of Human Destructiveness, Fromm writes:

"Destructiveness is the outcome of unlived life. Those who have ceased to grow inwardly, who feel powerless, respond with destruction—if I cannot create, I can at least destroy."

Are we not raised to think this way? Are we not educated in the path of dominance? From childhood, boys are told they must be strong, they must be fighters. The mother, the first and most influential figure in a child’s life, often reinforces this. If, at once, all mothers stopped praising boys for their aggression—if they taught their children that violence is neither honorable nor a measure of strength—perhaps wars would no longer exist. If no one was taught to be a bully, who would understand the need to be one?

As long as societies glorify conquest, as long as power is seen as dominance over others rather than mastery over oneself, the cycle of war will persist. But if we redefine strength—not as destruction, but as creation, not as control over others, but as control over oneself—perhaps humanity will evolve beyond its history of violence.

items.title: The engine of existence
items.header: The engine of existence is all around us—in the stars, in the quantum field, in the underlying fabric of reality. What we call free will may just be the illusion of choice within a predestined system, a system whose rules are hidden in the patterns of nature itself.
items.text:
Everything is connected. Our thoughts, actions, lives, and even destiny itself weave into a grand, intricate pattern—one we do not yet fully understand. As Hofstadter suggests,

"Strange loops arise when, by moving up or down through levels of some hierarchical system, we unexpectedly find ourselves right back where we started."

We live within such a loop, a program of learning, evolving, and self-reference.

I believe this world, in a sense, is a computer, a virtual reality of growth and experience, governed by principles we barely grasp. The engine of existence is all around us—in the stars, in the quantum field, in the underlying fabric of reality. What we call free will may just be the illusion of choice within a predestined system, a system whose rules are hidden in the patterns of nature itself.

The law of energy states that nothing truly disappears; it only changes form. The body we inhabit is but a loan from the earth, and when the time comes, it will return to the elements. As Carl Sagan once said,

“The nitrogen in our DNA, the calcium in our teeth, the iron in our blood, the carbon in our apple pies were made in the interiors of collapsing stars. We are made of star-stuff.”

And yet, beyond the physical, there is an underlying structure—the universal language of patterns. The harmonies of music, the motion of celestial bodies, the spectrum of light, and the mathematics of life itself all share the same fundamental diagrams and blueprints.

Consider the Golden Ratio, Fibonacci sequences, and fractals—echoed across galaxies, DNA strands, and the formation of shells and flowers. As Escher’s artwork shows, symmetry and recursion form the backbone of reality, much like a fugue in Bach’s compositions.

As Hofstadter writes,

“It turns out that an eerie type of chaos can lurk just behind a facade of order—and yet, deep inside the chaos lurks an even eerier type of order.”

The universe speaks in symbols—through light and shadow, numbers and rhythms, frequencies and vibrations. Pythagoras believed that all things are numbers, and quantum mechanics suggests that all existence is based on probability waves.

Thus, we are not separate from the cosmos, nor from each other. Every thought sends ripples into the quantum field. Every action is part of the grand equation. And in the end, we are but echoes of a greater melody, playing our notes in the infinite composition of existence.

items.title: The Cathars in a world of Rex Mundi
items.header: Once, the people lived. Lived at the sea, were intelligent, educated, religious. They had one sin – they were dualists – Christians who challenged the authority and teachings of the Roman Catholic Church.
items.text:
Once, the people lived. Lived at the sea, were intelligent, educated, religious. They had one sin – they were dualists – Christians who challenged the authority and teachings of the Roman Catholic Church.

Cathars lived in the Languedoc region (southern France) in cities like Albi, Carcassonne, and Montségur in the 12th–14th centuries. Men, women, and children were educated, knew several languages.

They believed in a form of dualism — that the world was a battleground between the forces of good (spiritual, immaterial) and evil (physical, material). They saw the physical world as corrupt, made by the God’s counterpart – false God. They rejected the Catholic Church's sacraments, wealth, and hierarchy.

And of course, the Catholic Church – the one who had to cherish the sacred soul and life – declared them heretics. In 1209, Pope Innocent III launched the Albigensian Crusade against them. This was one of the most brutal crusades in Europe.

Cathars were massacred, burned alive, executed for refusing to recant.

When the papal army attacked the city of Béziers, and soldiers asked how to distinguish Cathars from Catholics, the commander reportedly said:

“Kill them all; God will know His own.”

By the 14th century, the Cathars had been eradicated.

Everything we know about them comes from the inquisitor's journals – the last words of the developed civilisation's representatives.

Interesting thing is, the Pope who ordered their destruction was named Innocent.

He eradicated those who believed we were living in Hell.

Thinking of that... they were actually right, weren’t they? If this were truly a world of good, the Cathars would not have met such an end.

What we know of Hell to be – in flames with the demons – is just the subjective thought of our civilisation, precisely those who ruled it for centuries.

But why not Hell to be beautiful, admirable, full of possibilities for us to grow and evolve?

Think yourself – this world is full of illnesses, both physical and psychological. This world is full of pain, lies, addiction, ignorance, loss, betrayal, death. All this is part of our path here. Some become stronger, some – break down. This is the path of evolution, knowledge. As it is said – bad times make strong people.

But can all these awful things exist in a world that is truly good?

The Gnostics say that there are only two differences: lie / truth, material / ephemeral, chaos / order. We can continue: 0 / 1, bad / good, darkness / photon, hell / heaven, ignorance / consciousness.

Do you see it already?

So, where are we?
